> **Core services — shared runtime primitives**

---

# Cor — Claude Plugin Marketplace

**Cor** (Occitan: *heart*) is the plugin marketplace for the Occitan stack. It distributes agent definitions, multi-agent debate canvases, MCP tool servers, disciplines, practices, roles, and bundles of the above. Cor is also the umbrella repository for the Occitan stack: `.claude` config, cross-repo memory, and stack-level documentation all live here.

Default provider: **Claude** (Anthropic). The manifest schema is provider-agnostic; multi-provider declarations are supported by design.

## Repository Layout

```
Cor/
├── Cargo.toml                  # Rust workspace root (members: cor-core, cor-cli)
├── cor-core/                   # Rust library: manifest, registry, installer, lockfile, scaffold
├── cor-cli/                    # Rust binary: clap CLI wiring cor-core
├── packages/
│   ├── sdk/                    # @cor/sdk — TypeScript types and Zod validation
│   ├── registry/               # @cor/registry — Hono HTTP registry server
│   └── cli/                    # @cor/cli — TypeScript CLI (create/publish/install/search)
├── schemas/                    # JSON Schema drafts for plugin.json and config blocks
├── templates/                  # Scaffolding templates for each plugin type
├── docs/                       # Design specs and architecture notes
├── package.json                # pnpm workspace root
└── pnpm-workspace.yaml
```

---

## Dual-Workspace Architecture

Cor has two parallel workspaces that serve different use cases:

### Rust Workspace (`Cargo.toml`)

The canonical implementation. Designed for Occitan stack maintainers, CI pipelines, and plugin authors who want a fast native tool.

| Crate | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `cor-core` | Library: manifest parsing, registry index resolution, checksum verification, artifact fetching, lockfile I/O, scaffold code |
| `cor-cli` | Binary (`cor`): clap-based CLI that wires `cor-core` into commands |

Key dependencies: `tokio` (async), `clap` (CLI), `serde`+`toml` (manifests/lockfile), `reqwest` (artifact fetching), `sha2`+`hex` (checksums), `thiserror`/`anyhow` (error handling).

### TypeScript Workspace (`pnpm-workspace.yaml`)

An npm-ecosystem-native counterpart, better suited for plugin authors in JS/TS projects and for running the registry HTTP server.

| Package | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `@cor/sdk` | Shared TypeScript types and Zod validation schemas; no runtime dependencies beyond `zod` |
| `@cor/registry` | Hono HTTP server that exposes a REST API over a flat JSON file store |
| `@cor/cli` | `cor` binary for Node.js: interactive `create`, `publish`, `install`, `search` |

Both workspaces share the same plugin manifest semantics. The Rust side uses TOML (`plugin.toml`); the TypeScript side uses JSON (`plugin.json`).

---

## Plugin Types

| Kind | Description | Default install target |
|---|---|---|
| `agent` | System prompt + persona + optional tool list | `Fondament/definitions/roles/installed/` |
| `mcp` | MCP tool server (stdio / SSE / HTTP transport) | `Fondament/definitions/tools/installed/` |
| `debate-canvas` / `canvas` | Multi-agent debate configuration | `Amassada/canvases/installed/` |
| `discipline` | Occitan discipline definition | `Fondament/definitions/disciplines/installed/` |
| `practice` | Occitan practice definition | `Fondament/definitions/practices/installed/` |
| `role` | Role definition (alias of agent in Rust) | `Fondament/definitions/roles/installed/` |
| `bundle` | Collection of atomic plugins declared as dependencies | `plugins/installed/` |

Bundles have no artifact of their own; they declare `[dependencies]` which the installer resolves individually.

---

## Plugin Manifest Format

### plugin.toml (Rust/TOML)

Every Rust-side plugin ships a `plugin.toml` at its root. Two kinds: **atomic** (single artifact) and **bundle** (dependency collection).

#### Atomic plugin

```toml
[plugin]
id = "design-session"
version = "1.2.0"
kind = "canvas"           # canvas | agent | mcp | discipline | practice | role
name = "Design Session Canvas"
description = "Architecture design session — produces ADR and component artifacts"
authors = ["acme-org"]
license = "MIT"
repository = "https://github.com/acme/design-session-plugin"

[compatibility]
stack = ">=0.1.0"
providers = ["claude"]    # declare ["claude", "gemini"] for multi-provider

[artifact]
path = "canvases/design-session.yaml"   # relative path inside the plugin repo

[install]
target = "Amassada/canvases/installed/" # path relative to consuming repo root
                                        # omit to use the kind default
```

#### Bundle plugin

```toml
[plugin]
id = "jira-integration"
version = "2.0.0"
kind = "bundle"
name = "Jira Integration"
description = "Full Jira integration: discipline, MCP, capability, and triage canvas"
authors = ["acme-org"]
license = "MIT"
repository = "https://github.com/acme/jira-integration-plugin"

[compatibility]
stack = ">=0.1.0"
providers = ["claude"]

[dependencies]
plugins = [
  "jira-discipline@^1.0",
  "jira-mcp@^2.0",
  "jira-triage-canvas@^1.0",
]
```

#### Manifest structs (`cor-core/src/manifest.rs`)

```
Plugin
  plugin: PluginMeta      (id, version, kind, name, description, authors, license, repository)
  compatibility: Compatibility  (stack semver, providers[])
  artifact?: ArtifactDef  (path)
  install?: InstallDef    (target)
  dependencies?: DependencyDef  (plugins[])
```

`Plugin::install_target()` returns the `[install].target` value if present, otherwise falls back to `default_install_target()` based on kind.

Atomic plugins must have `[artifact]`; bundle plugins must have `[dependencies]` and no `[artifact]`. `cor publish` enforces both rules.

### plugin.json (TypeScript/JSON)

The TypeScript side uses JSON manifests validated by Zod schemas in `@cor/sdk`.

```json
{
  "id": "my-org/my-agent",
  "name": "My Agent",
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "description": "Does something useful",
  "type": "agent",
  "author": { "name": "My Org", "email": "hi@example.com" },
  "main": "agent.json",
  "tags": ["productivity"],
  "dependencies": { "my-org/some-mcp": "^1.0.0" },
  "config": {
    "systemPrompt": "You are a helpful assistant.",
    "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6",
    "tools": ["my-org/some-mcp"],
    "persona": { "name": "My Agent" }
  }
}
```

**ID format:** `scope/name` — both parts must match `[a-z0-9-]+`. For example: `acme/code-reviewer`.

**Version:** standard semver — `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH` with optional pre-release and build metadata.

The `config` object is type-specific:

| `type` | Config fields |
|---|---|
| `agent` | `systemPrompt` (required), `model`, `tools`, `temperature`, `maxTokens`, `persona` |
| `mcp` | `transport` (required: `stdio`/`sse`/`http`), `command`, `args`, `env`, `url`, `tools[]` |
| `debate-canvas` | `topic` (required), `agents[]` (min 2, each with `pluginId`+`role`), `rounds` (required), `moderatorPluginId` |

---

## Registry

### Git-Based Index (cor-registry)

The primary registry is a separate git repository (`cor-registry`). No custom server is required — git is the registry. Index entries are TOML files that act as pointers; the actual artifacts stay in each plugin's own repo.

```
cor-registry/
└── index/
    ├── canvases/    design-session.toml
    ├── agents/      bpa-analyst.toml
    ├── mcps/        jira-mcp.toml
    ├── bundles/     jira-integration.toml
    ├── disciplines/
    ├── practices/
    └── roles/
```

Each index entry (`IndexEntry` in Rust):

```toml
# cor-registry/index/canvases/design-session.toml
id = "design-session"
latest = "1.2.0"
repository = "https://github.com/acme/design-session-plugin"
checksum = "sha256:abc123..."
```

`RegistryIndex::from_dir()` reads the entire index directory tree. `find(id)` does an exact-match lookup; `search(query)` does a case-insensitive substring match on IDs.

Publishing is a PR workflow:

```
cor publish
  → validate plugin.toml
  → print the index entry to open as a PR to cor-registry
  → maintainer merges → plugin is discoverable
```

### HTTP Registry Server

`@cor/registry` is a Hono HTTP server for the TypeScript ecosystem. It serves JSON manifests from a local flat-file store (`data/plugins.json`).

**Endpoints:**

| Method | Path | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `GET` | `/health` | Health check — returns `{ status: "ok", ts: "..." }` |
| `GET` | `/plugins` | List/search plugins. Query params: `q` (text search), `type` (filter), `page` (default 1, page size 20) |
| `GET` | `/plugins/:id` | Fetch latest version of a plugin by ID. Optional `?version=x.y.z` |
| `POST` | `/plugins` | Publish a plugin. Multipart body with `manifest` field (JSON string or object). Returns 201 on success, 400 with validation issues on failure. |

Search matches against `id`, `name`, `description`, and `tags`.

The store (`packages/registry/src/store/index.ts`) maintains a versioned index — multiple versions per plugin ID, returned latest-first by `publishedAt`. Downloads are incremented on every `GET /:id` hit.

**Running the registry:**

```bash
pnpm --filter @cor/registry dev    # dev mode with tsx watch
pnpm --filter @cor/registry start  # compiled
# Defaults to port 3000; override with PORT env var
```

---

## SDK — Types and Validation

`@cor/sdk` (`packages/sdk/`) is the shared types-and-validation layer. It has no runtime dependencies beyond `zod`.

### Types (`src/types.ts`)

```typescript
PluginType       = 'agent' | 'mcp' | 'debate-canvas'
McpTransport     = 'stdio' | 'sse' | 'http'

PluginAuthor     { name, email?, url? }
McpToolDef       { name, description, inputSchema }
AgentConfig      { systemPrompt, model?, tools?, temperature?, maxTokens?, persona? }
McpConfig        { transport, command?, args?, env?, url?, tools[] }
DebateAgentRef   { pluginId, role }
DebateCanvasConfig { topic, agents[], rounds, moderatorPluginId? }

PluginManifest   { id, name, version, description, type, author, main, tags?, dependencies?, config? }
RegistryPlugin   extends PluginManifest + { publishedAt, updatedAt, downloads, checksum, archiveUrl }
SearchResult     { plugins[], total, page, pageSize }
```

### Zod Schemas (`src/validate.ts`)

```typescript
import { PluginManifestSchema, AgentConfigSchema, McpConfigSchema, DebateCanvasConfigSchema } from '@cor/sdk';

const result = PluginManifestSchema.safeParse(raw);
if (!result.success) {
  result.error.issues.forEach(i => console.error(i.path.join('.'), i.message));
}
```

Validation rules:
- `id` must match `^[a-z0-9-]+/[a-z0-9-]+$`
- `version` must be valid semver
- `author.email` must be a valid email address if present
- `author.url` must be a valid URL if present
- `DebateCanvasConfig.agents` requires at least 2 entries
- `AgentConfig.temperature` must be in `[0, 1]`
- `McpConfig.tools` is required (may be empty array)

`ValidatedManifest` is the inferred Zod output type: `z.infer<typeof PluginManifestSchema>`.

---

## Rust CLI (cor)

Build and install:

```bash
cargo build --release
# Binary: target/release/cor
```

### Commands

#### `cor new <kind> <name>`

Scaffold a new plugin directory with `plugin.toml` and a sample artifact file.

```bash
cor new agent my-assistant
# Creates: my-assistant/plugin.toml
#          my-assistant/roles/my-assistant.yaml
```

Supported kinds: `canvas`, `agent`, `mcp`, `discipline`, `practice`, `role`, `bundle`.

The generated `plugin.toml` is fully filled in with the correct `[artifact]` path and `[install] target` for the chosen kind. For bundles, no artifact is created — a `bundle.yaml` placeholder is written instead.

#### `cor install [id] [--registry <path>] [--dry-run]`

Install a plugin. Three install sources:

```bash
cor install design-session              # from registry index
cor install ./my-local-plugin           # from local path (reads plugin.toml)
cor install                             # reinstall all from cor.lock
```

The registry path defaults to `cor-registry/index`. Clone `cor-registry` first (or pass `--registry` to point at a local clone).

With `--dry-run`, all steps are logged but no files are written and `cor.lock` is not updated.

On registry install: looks up the `IndexEntry`, fetches the artifact from the source repository at the pinned version tag, verifies the SHA-256 checksum, writes the file to the install target, and updates `cor.lock`.

Artifact URL construction:
- GitHub: `{repo}/raw/v{version}/{artifact_path}`
- GitLab: `{repo}/-/raw/v{version}/{artifact_path}`
- Other: `{source_base}/{artifact_path}`

#### `cor publish [--dry-run]`

Validate `plugin.toml` in the current directory and print the index entry needed to open a PR to `cor-registry`.

Validation checks:
- `plugin.id` is non-empty
- `plugin.version` is non-empty
- `plugin.repository` is non-empty
- Atomic plugins have `[artifact]`
- Bundle plugins have `[dependencies]`

With `--dry-run`, stops after validation.

#### `cor search <query> [--registry <path>]`

Search the registry index by plugin ID (case-insensitive substring match). Prints matching IDs, versions, and repository URLs.

#### `cor list`

List all installed plugins from `cor.lock` in the current directory.

#### `cor validate`

Parse and validate `plugin.toml` in the current directory. Prints the ID, kind, and version on success; exits non-zero on parse error.

#### `cor info <id> [--registry <path>]`

Print full registry metadata for a plugin: ID, latest version, repository, and checksum.

---

## TypeScript CLI (@cor/cli)

The TypeScript CLI is an npm-installable `cor` binary built with `commander`. It talks to the HTTP registry server.

```bash
pnpm --filter @cor/cli build
node packages/cli/dist/index.js --help

# Or in dev mode:
pnpm --filter @cor/cli dev
```

Registry URL defaults to `http://localhost:3000`; override with the `COR_REGISTRY` environment variable or the `-r / --registry` flag.

### Commands

#### `cor create [name]`

Interactive scaffolding wizard. Prompts for:
- Plugin ID in `scope/name` format
- Display name
- Plugin type (`agent`, `mcp`, or `debate-canvas`)
- Description
- Author name and email

Reads the corresponding template from `templates/<type>/plugin.json`, performs `{{variable}}` substitution, and writes `plugin.json` to a new directory named after the slug.

#### `cor publish [-r <registry>]`

Validates `plugin.json` in the current directory using `PluginManifestSchema` and POSTs it to `POST /plugins` on the registry. Exits non-zero with detailed Zod issue output on validation failure.

#### `cor install <plugin> [-r <registry>] [-d <dir>]`

Installs a plugin from the registry. `<plugin>` may include a version pin: `scope/name@1.2.3`. Fetches from `GET /plugins/:id` (with optional `?version=`), writes `plugin.json` to `<dir>/<id>/` (default: `.cor/plugins/<id>/`).

#### `cor search [query] [-r <registry>] [-t <type>] [-p <page>]`

Searches the registry. Results are color-coded by type (agent = blue, mcp = magenta, debate-canvas = yellow) and include download counts.

---

## Lockfile (cor.lock)

`cor.lock` is a TOML file committed to the consuming repository. It pins exact versions and checksums for all installed plugins, enabling reproducible installs (`cor install` with no arguments reinstalls everything).

```toml
[[package]]
id = "design-session"
version = "1.2.0"
source = "https://github.com/acme/design-session-plugin"
checksum = "sha256:abc123..."
install_target = "Amassada/canvases/installed/"
kind = "canvas"

[[package]]
id = "jira-mcp"
version = "2.1.0"
source = "https://github.com/acme/jira-mcp-plugin"
checksum = "sha256:def456..."
install_target = "Fondament/definitions/tools/installed/"
kind = "mcp"
```

`CorLock` (`cor-core/src/lockfile.rs`) reads and writes this file. `add()` upserts by ID (removes the old entry first); `remove()` drops it. The file is only written after a successful install.

---

## Templates

The TypeScript CLI uses templates from `templates/<type>/plugin.json`. Each template uses `{{variable}}` placeholders substituted during `cor create`.

| Template | File | Variables |
|---|---|---|
| `agent` | `templates/agent/plugin.json` | `scope`, `name`, `displayName`, `description`, `authorName`, `authorEmail` |
| `mcp` | `templates/mcp/plugin.json` | same |
| `debate-canvas` | `templates/debate-canvas/plugin.json` | same |

The agent template starts with `claude-sonnet-4-6` as the default model. The MCP template defaults to `stdio` transport with `node server.js`. The debate-canvas template defaults to 2 agents (advocate + critic) and 3 rounds.

The Rust CLI (`cor new`) generates `plugin.toml` directly from `cor-core/src/scaffold.rs` without reading the JSON templates. Both produce equivalent semantics.

---

## Install Targets — Fondament and Amassada

Cor installs artifacts into the correct directory of the consuming Occitan stack project. Two sibling repositories are relevant:

- **Fondament** — the definitions layer (agents, disciplines, practices, roles, MCP tool configs)
- **Amassada** — the canvas layer (multi-agent debate configurations)

Default install paths per kind:

| Kind | Path |
|---|---|
| `canvas` | `Amassada/canvases/installed/` |
| `agent` / `role` | `Fondament/definitions/roles/installed/` |
| `discipline` | `Fondament/definitions/disciplines/installed/` |
| `practice` | `Fondament/definitions/practices/installed/` |
| `mcp` | `Fondament/definitions/tools/installed/` |
| `bundle` | `plugins/installed/` |

These paths are relative to the root of whichever repository runs `cor install`. Override with `[install] target` in `plugin.toml`.

Installed files are typically gitignored (they are generated from `cor.lock`). Teams may choose to commit them.

---

## JSON Schemas

`schemas/` contains JSON Schema Draft-07 definitions for tooling integration (editor validation, CI linting):

| File | `$id` | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `plugin.schema.json` | `https://cor.occitan/schemas/plugin.schema.json` | Top-level `plugin.json` manifest |
| `agent.schema.json` | `https://cor.occitan/schemas/agent.schema.json` | `config` block for `type: agent` |
| `mcp.schema.json` | `https://cor.occitan/schemas/mcp.schema.json` | `config` block for `type: mcp` |
| `debate-canvas.schema.json` | `https://cor.occitan/schemas/debate-canvas.schema.json` | `config` block for `type: debate-canvas` |

Add to your editor's JSON schema associations to get inline validation as you edit `plugin.json`.

---

## Dev Setup

### Prerequisites

- Rust stable (1.75+)
- Node.js 20+
- pnpm 9+

### Rust

```bash
# Build everything
cargo build

# Build release binary
cargo build --release

# Run the CLI directly
cargo run -p cor -- --help
cargo run -p cor -- new agent my-agent
cargo run -p cor -- validate
```

### TypeScript

```bash
# Install dependencies
pnpm install

# Build all packages (sdk first, then cli and registry)
pnpm build

# Start the registry in dev mode (tsx watch)
pnpm dev

# Start the TypeScript CLI in dev mode
pnpm --filter @cor/cli dev -- --help
```

### Environment Variables

| Variable | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `PORT` | `3000` | Registry server port |
| `COR_REGISTRY` | `http://localhost:3000` | Registry URL used by the TypeScript CLI |

### Workspace Scripts (root `package.json`)

```bash
pnpm build    # build all TypeScript packages
pnpm dev      # start @cor/registry in watch mode
pnpm test     # run tests across all packages
pnpm lint     # lint all packages
```

---

## Testing

### Rust

```bash
cargo test                    # run all unit tests
cargo test -p cor-core        # test the library only
```

The core logic to test: manifest parsing round-trips, checksum verification, lockfile add/remove, `RegistryIndex::search`, and `resolve_install_path`.

### TypeScript

```bash
pnpm test                     # run all package tests
pnpm --filter @cor/sdk test   # SDK validation tests
```

The SDK's Zod schemas are the primary validation surface; test both valid and invalid manifests against `PluginManifestSchema.safeParse`.

### Manual end-to-end (Rust CLI)

```bash
# Scaffold a plugin
cargo run -p cor -- new agent test-agent

# Validate it
cd test-agent && cargo run -p cor -- validate

# Publish dry-run
cargo run -p cor -- publish --dry-run
```

### Manual end-to-end (TypeScript)

```bash
# Start the registry
pnpm dev &

# In another shell — create, publish, search, install
pnpm --filter @cor/cli dev -- create my-org/my-agent
cd my-agent
pnpm --filter @cor/cli dev -- publish
pnpm --filter @cor/cli dev -- search my-agent
pnpm --filter @cor/cli dev -- install my-org/my-agent
```
